question: Create a base class `Vehicle` with private members `brand` (string) and `year` (int). Implement a constructor and a public method `displayInfo()`. Derive a `Car` class and a `Motorcycle` class from `Vehicle`. Each derived class should add one specific private attribute (e.g., `numDoors` for `Car`, `hasSidecar` for `Motorcycle`) and override `displayInfo()` to include its specific attribute.

test_cases.expected:
--- Calling displayInfo via Base Pointers (Semantic Error) ---
Brand: Toyota, Year: 2020, Doors: 4
Brand: Harley-Davidson, Year: 2022, Has Sidecar: Yes

--- Calling displayInfo directly on Derived Objects (Correct) ---
Brand: Toyota, Year: 2020, Doors: 4
Brand: Harley-Davidson, Year: 2022, Has Sidecar: Yes
test_cases.actual_output_pattern:
--- Calling displayInfo via Base Pointers \(Semantic Error\) ---
Vehicle Base Info - Brand: Toyota, Year: 2020

Vehicle Base Info - Brand: Harley-Davidson, Year: 2022

--- Calling displayInfo directly on Derived Objects \(Correct\) ---
Vehicle Base Info - Brand: Toyota, Year: 2020, Doors: 4
Vehicle Base Info - Brand: Harley-Davidson, Year: 2022, Has Sidecar: Yes

errors: The `displayInfo()` method in the `Vehicle` base class is not declared `virtual`. When `displayInfo()` is called through base class pointers (`Vehicle* v1 = &myCar; v1->displayInfo();`), the base class version of the method is invoked instead of the derived class version, preventing polymorphic behavior. This violates the principle of polymorphism for overridden methods.
